For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    print(f"id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE only changed the green bar (from 56 to 53 pixels, shrinking by 3 this time instead of 2). The blue bar didn't move. So SPACE is just a wasted action that advances the timer faster. I should avoid it.
Now let me think about the goal. The blue bar is at rows 16-19, cols 12-31. The yellow shapes are at the bottom. Let me look more carefully at the yellow shapes - they seem to have "holes" or openings. The left yellow shape boundary shows it goes from row 52-59, with cols 16-27. The shape has an opening at the top (rows 52-55) that's narrower.
Looking at the boundary more carefully for the left yellow:
- [[52, 16], [52, 19], [55, 19], [56, 20], [56, 23], [55, 24], [52, 24], [52, 27], [59, 27], [59, 16]]
This traces: top-left (52,16) -> (52,19) -> goes down to (55,19) -> (56,20) -> (56,23) -> up to (55,24) -> (52,24) -> (52,27) -> down to (59,27) -> (59,16) -> back up.
So the shape has a "notch" or indentation in the middle at rows 55-56, cols 20-23. The top part (rows 52-55) has two columns: cols 16-19 and cols 24-27, with a gap at cols 20-23.
The blue bar is 20 columns wide (cols 12-31) and 4 rows tall. It's much wider than the yellow shapes.
Wait, maybe the goal isn't about fitting into the yellow shapes. Let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is about color matching or pattern completion. Or maybe I need to move the blue bar to a specific position to "paint" or "fill" something.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole puzzle. The "39 target actions" hint suggests this level needs about 39 actions. If each arrow key press moves the bar 4 cells, and the bar needs to traverse the board... 
Let me think differently. Maybe the goal is to move the blue bar around to "paint" the orange background blue, filling it in. Each movement would leave a trail. Let me test this by moving the bar down and seeing if it leaves a trail.
Actually wait - let me re-examine. The blue bar is 4x20 = 80 pixels. When it moves, does it leave a trail or does it just move? From the earlier observations, when I moved RIGHT, the blue bar moved from cols 12-31 to cols 16-35, and the cells changed count was 34 (which is 4*20 - 4*6 = 80-46 = 34... no that's not right). Actually 34 = 4*4 + 4*4 + 2 = the new cells on the right minus overlap. Let me recalculate: moving right by 4, the bar was at 12-31 and moved to 16-35. The overlap is cols 16-31 (16 cols), so 4*16=64 cells stay the same. New cells on right: 4*4=16. Cells vacated on left: 4*4=16. Total changed: 16+16=32. But it reported 34... hmm, maybe the HUD bar also changed (2 cells).
So the bar just moves without leaving a trail. The goal must be something else.
Let me think about what 39 actions could mean. If the bar needs to visit all the yellow shapes or something... Or maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on things?
Let me try using MOUSE on the yellow shapes to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on the left yellow shape with MOUSE
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 20}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
